And certainly we see in neurology that disturbances of language, and that's what aphasia means.
To have an aphasic disorder means A, again, is the loss of phasic ability to communicate or ability to engage in communicative behavior.
And aphasic disorders, they're extremely common. At least they're extremely common right after a person has a neurological insult.
So, especially if you have a stroke, about 40% of the people who have a stroke are going to have some sort of communication problem.
It's going to be the thing, one of two things is going to happen when you know you've had a stroke.
You're going to wake up and you're going to feel like you're weak, or might not even wake up.
You might just suddenly feel dizzy and feel you have weakness.
And you might try to speak and be unable to speak.
Or suddenly you don't understand what people are saying to you.
Now again, that's what we call the acute phase. Right after you have the stroke, you're going to have these aphasic disorders.
Fortunately, in 40% of the individuals, if we think about that, half the strokes are in the right hemisphere, half are in the left hemisphere.

hemisphere, and it's much more common in the left hemisphere, as we'll talk about, that it means that if you have a stroke or some disturbance in the left hemisphere, you're probably going to have some disruption of language, at least in the early days after the stroke, when everything is a bit chaotic in terms of just sort of the whole balance of function in the left hemisphere is distorted.
And in many cases, people are going to recover, so there's going to be great variation in the severity of the aphasic disorder, and especially in the recovery.
Person might not be able to speak immediately after the stroke, a week later, they might be perfectly fine again, completely have recovered.

Now, the study of aphasia really is kind of the classic cases that are cited in the neurology or neuropsychology literature, and it really stemmed from these 19th century observations of Broca and Wernicke, and again, many of you probably heard about them in other classes.

And again, people understood clearly by the 19th century that the brain was central to our mental capabilities, but these guys did this work about making localization, making analysis showing that there was a relationship between behavior and damage to particular parts of the brain.

So Broca, in around 1860 or 1860, published a paper about a patient who seemed to actually understand language, okay, you could sort of have a conversation with him, at least he seemed to, you know, be tuned in to the person who was speaking to him, kind of maybe respond in at least appropriate manner of turn-taking, but all he would say was, tan, tan, tan, and I'll actually show you a patient like this in a few minutes.

So he clearly had a problem, but he couldn't say, he had no variation in what he produced, and again, it'd be obviously hard to know, is the person really understanding you if you just, if all they ever said to you was the word tan back at you, but they could give this guy commands and he could respond to the commands to show other ways that he understood that he was comprehending language.

Shortly afterwards though, Wernicke described a bunch of patients who actually seemed to have a different picture, that their language production was still intact, but their language comprehension was severely compromised, and both of them had access to the brains of the patients they had studied after they had died, so they'd done autopsies, and they'd actually been able to look and see where was the damage, and what was striking is that the patient Broca was describing had damage in this inferior frontal region here, Wernicke's patients tended to have lesions more posterior, especially on the superior parts of the temporal lobe, and of course, that's why these two areas get their common names that are still referred to by as Broca's area and Wernicke's area, and in the classic neurology model, it was a simple story, you know, Wernicke's area sits close to the primary auditory cortex, this was thought to be the seed of language comprehension, Broca's area, the seed of language production, indeed, it was identified that there was this arcuate fasciculus, that's just a white matter track, but it's a very prominent one, so it has this particular name, arcuate fasciculus, and it actually is very prominent in humans, and it goes directly from Wernicke's area to Broca's area, and the thought was that with evolutionary pressures, there had to be this very strong communication between the receptive parts of language and the productive part of language, so again, in your classic behavioral neurology picture, that's sort of what the story might have looked like, say, in circa 1950, but as we'll see over the next few lectures, the story's much more complex, much more nuanced.

This, though, is a cool video from about 15 years ago now from Bob Knight's lab, it's a patient who, again, has severe epilepsy, they're going to do some surgery to try to take out the tissue that's generating the epileptic seizures here, so they've implanted this grid of electrodes on the cortical surface, and basically the patient's going to be in the hospital for about a week prior to the surgery, where they're just recording from the electrodes clinically to identify where the seizure activity originates, but while they're in the hospital, they could do experiments, they could say, what activates the electrodes?

Here's just a movie of the patient doing a very simple task, they hear a word spoken to them or presented on the computer screen, they hear the word, no, they actually hear the word presented, and their job is to hear a noun, and they just have to say a verb that's associated with it, so they might hear the word knife, and they have to say cut, or they might hear the word chair, and they have to say sit, and what you're just going to see here is that how the activity traverses across these different electrode sites, so again, as the electrodes increase, they're of course all firing all the time, but as they increase their firing, you'll actually see it play out in real time up at the top, so there's the stimulus being presented, again, right back here in Wernicke's area, then the person's processing it, we're seeing some frontal lobe activation, along then these premotor regions here, and now the person's making the response up close to Broca's area, and again, I'll just play that one more time, now that you're oriented, so this is just going to be taking just going to be taking place.
2,000 milliseconds is two seconds. So this is in real time now.
Again, right at zero, the word's presented. Immediately activation in Wernicke's area.
And then as the person's doing this verb retrieval, right, they're thinking about what word to respond.
You're seeing this frontal activation, and now you see the response.
When they respond, they're speaking out loud, and you again see some activation back in Wernicke's area, perhaps hearing their own voice speaking there.
So again, very consistent with the classic picture that we see there.
All right. Well, of course, one of the real things that came out of the lessons from Broca and Wernicke is that we saw this strong lateralization.
Their patient's a small sample, right? Broca really dealing with one patient, Wernicke with a handful of patients.
All the damage there on the left hemisphere, but very quickly it became apparent that language was strongly lateralized to the left hemisphere.
And I'll just walk you through some of the evidence for that.
This is a large-scale study that was published a couple years ago.
Basically, what they did is it's an fMRI study. They devised a series of tasks, very short tasks that they thought could isolate different types of cognitive functions.
They were mainly interested in those tasks that identified what they called the essential components of language.
So it involved comprehension. It might involve speaking. It was mostly about comprehension.

They were trying to separate, you know, more complex functions that might be involved in manipulating language, but they just wanted to look at if people were reading a simple word, listening to a word, having to repeat the word, what brain areas were engaged.

And the main point they want to make here is that across these 45 different languages, and again, some of them have been isolated from one another for say 50,000 years of different evolutionary branches, you see this very common network across all these brains here.
And again, it's strongly lateralized to the left hemisphere. I'm actually only showing you left hemisphere brains.
So again, these are different language sub-families and then within them, different sub-specialties, specializations, or specific languages within these different branches.
But their picture here is that something's involved in our brains that actually has formed this essential language network.
Doesn't matter what language you learn, you're going to be engaging a very similar set of brain structures.

On the right here, it's just making the point that if you actually look over to the right hemisphere, you can either look in terms of the percent bold signal, so the strength of the fMRI activation during the language test, or you can look at it in terms of how many voxels, that is right when we do fMRI studies, we're having these volumetric units that we can measure the activity within.
So you can count up the number of voxels that respond during this language, that are part of this language network, or the strength of the activation.

You can look at this two ways. One way is that clearly there is a stronger left hemisphere activation, more voxels being activated, but at the same time, you're also seeing parallel activation in the right hemisphere.
So it's not really left hemisphere almost for language, but it also, we look from this picture here, that there's also considerable right hemisphere engagement during these same language tests.

I'm not sure what it means. In terms of evidence from brain damage, obviously, we're going to talk about lots of patients with aphasic disorders, almost always associated with left hemisphere damage.

One thing... One observation that was made back in the 70s and 80s was that you could actually take people who were born without spoken language, who used sign language, and you could ask when they had brain damage, did they show similar patterns as what you saw in speaking individuals?
And indeed, they did. So people who use purely sign language, completely deaf from birth, if they have a posterior limb damage, they tend to have trouble interpreting sign language.
If they have an anterior lesion, a frontal lobe lesion, they have trouble producing sign language.
So a very similar pattern to what you saw in healthy individuals.
A real question, though, is what about the recovery? As I mentioned, people can have strokes, have really severe aphasic disorders, but fortunately, many people recover.
Certainly, some individuals will have chronic problems, aphasic disorders, and it's really something that really impacts the quality of their life.
But most people recover a good deal of their language functions, and of course, there's lots of work devoted to doing language rehabilitation.
We'll talk about that a little bit later on, too.

But the question is, when you recover, is it that, remember I said, there's some activation in the right hemisphere, if your left hemisphere is damaged, does recovery rely on the reorganization of the right hemisphere?
Is the right hemisphere going to take over those language functions, or is there actually reorganization or recruitment of the spare tissue in the left hemisphere?
And so people have asked that by tracking individuals across the period of recovery.
So these are all individuals who had some reasonable degree of recovery of speech, where they're scanned at three different points.
Right after the stroke, that's the acute phase. In a subacute phase, where they're still having pretty severe problems, but they're making some recovery.
And back in the chronic stage, so this would be about six months after the stroke, when they've kind of reached a plateau.
They've recovered a lot of their language functions, there might be some still residual problems here.
And again, this is basically showing, this is the control pattern that we've seen.
There's the language network, a little bit of activation in the right.
A strong left hemisphere lateralization. In the acute phase, of course, this is when the people are severely compromised in their language abilities.
Almost no activation in the right hemisphere, very little activation in the left.

In the subacute phase, now what you see is actually pretty strong activation in the right hemisphere, especially in these anterior regions, but also some recovery going on in corresponding regions of the left hemisphere.
And then the interesting thing is that in the chronic phase, that right hemisphere recruitment has actually died down, so that now they're actually looking very much like controls.
So it really seems to be that it's not that the right hemisphere takes over for the damaged left hemisphere.

I'm not showing you here the lesions. These individuals are going to have large lesions in the left hemisphere, but it's rather that the residual tissue in the left hemisphere reorganizes and reasserts itself over that period of the recovery.

There is a raging debate to this day. So here is Broca's area.
It's on the outer surface of the inferior frontal lobe.
And here was a study that was done to, say, take 25 patients who had Broca's-like symptoms and ask where was the damage most likely to occur.
And of course, they expected it was going to occur in Broca's area.

But when they did the analysis, and again, the color coding here is the percent of overlap, it turns out that the one place that was affected in all 25 patients was not Broca's area, but it was actually medial, buried deep inside there in what's called the insula region of the frontal lobe.

And so the argument has been made that what we classically thought was Broca's area is kind of a misnomer, that Broca's aphasia is actually more associated with damage to these medial or more interior regions of the frontal lobe rather than the outer region.

Now, the debate that goes on is, well, you know, it may be that the blood supply is more likely to damage this interior region, but it doesn't necessarily mean that the outer region isn't the critical part for the production of language.
And that's an ongoing debate, but it did inspire Lena Drunkard, the Berkeley researcher who had worked on the patient we just saw in the video, to actually go over to France.
